elements.text:
It's been really interesting to me that we still haven't seen multi-agent systems take off, even with how powerful a single agent system like Claude Code is, and it's got me thinking that we might be dealing with something different and need a new programming/computing environment to explore it...

So...I've been playing around with this idea of taking some of the core original ideas of OO/Smalltalk (Objects as self contained computers that pass messages with binding at late as possible) and seeing what we could do if we added an LLM in there...and have landed on this concept of "Prompt-Objects" that are self-contained computers (essentially what people call agents) that have plaintext instructions, have access to tools as primitives, and run in a loop with an LLM, can pass messages to each other (or to humans) in natural language, and basically no binding (or essentially semantic late binding(?)).

So far I've got a simple environment set up where you can interact with these prompt objects, create new ones, and trace their communication with each other...but I'm curious if anyone else has gone down this line or thinking or gives you any ideas? I recently rewatched Kay's "
The Computer Revolution Hasn't Happened Yet

" and have been toying around with other biological inspired ideas like environmental/pheromone-style signaling...but that's down the line

I played around with it a bit this weekend and it might just be me, but it's actually been kind of easier to think of different "agentic" flows by not really thinking of them as "agents" and thinking of them as "prompt objects" that you build a program with...

text:
There are many things concerning me with the „AI“ development. obviously as a software guy yegge seems disinterested in hardware or energy costs. 

„obviously“ he assumes everyone is already running at least 20-30 agents - full time for his daily work. 

Do you feel a sense of disconnect , for example from energy costs ?

for now all this is running and accelerating on coal and other fossil that was spun up in addition to regenerative. 

to keep the pace silicon valley is already promising nuclear (terra power gates etc. )

====> Finally my questions:

What happens if the nuclear energy is not sufficiently provided ? does the AI bubble religion collapse ?

what happens if the energy - somehow - is provided?

Am I taking this to serious? :sweat_smile:
or are these just pathetic rumblings before …

what happens if you don't look at the code anymore ?

text: compiler writers still look at generated code. Its just not part of the daily workflow except when important. I think that might be true in the future too.

text:
• programming was invented when we stopped using rats' nests of transistors and switched to using opcodes to build new things - programming productivity increased
• programming productivity increased when we stopped using assembler and switched to "high level languages" (like C).
• few people bother to look at the layout of transistors in ICs, few people bother to look at the layout of opcodes in the binaries they produce - they use their freed up time in new ways

text:
<https://www.1a-insec.net/blog/20-janet-graphical-repl/|Janet REPL in the image of Self and gtoolkit> — each evaluation pops a window with value + prompt for doing more computations with `_` <https://codeberg.org/iacore/janet-graphical-repl/src/commit/102ed55cc91268f161d6ed0f431c9d4e71b686a3/src/main.zig#L208|bound to that value>.
I don't think randomly placed windows will scale to long sessions as well as say notebooks, but I do like the non-linear idea of every value having its own REPL prompt :thinking_face:

text:
Thanks for sharing the article! We’re living in crazy times.

I think what disturbs me a bit about the whole thing is that I thought that the end of the road was a better high level programming, not natural language — let alone, not very well written natural language (since models can more or less figure out what you meant).

text:
<@UJFN50C00> you are not at all alone for your energy concerns, I'm in the middle of a big letter about other stuff, but this is a paragraph from the disclaimer at the beginning.  Seems a good time to share...

Many people are discussing important and pressing questions raised by recent developments in AI.  Outside this disclaimer, I will not be among them. ... [then a few paragraphs later] ...

To learn more about AI energy use, listen to [Science Vs](<https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/ai-is-it-ruining-the-environment/id1051557000?i=1000736569496>) or this Hank Green [rant](<https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=H_c6MWk7PQc>).  I would rant too except I know that when friends bring it up they're sincerely concerned.  Remember most things have costs, weigh benefits too, include opportunity costs.  What's more, the equation is constantly changing in the promising direction and not by a little, factors of hundreds, [390x per year](<https://www.deeplearning.ai/the-batch/issue-332/>) for GPT doing the ARC-AGI-1 test.  Yes, data-center deployment raises resource allocation questions: who gets the benefit, who bears the cost, with what compensation.  AI is new, not novel.

text: <@U02U0AS3J49> Seriously!  Vibe coding a one-off toy is one thing, but if you want to use it for something that you're not going to personally debug where are your acceptance tests?  What is you quality assurance protocol?  Have you ever done software engineering?  Managed a team of ten or more?  The techniques are immediately and directly applicable.  Sucks to be promoted out of your artisanal comfort zone, but nothing you now need to know is new.

text:
<@U0887QU9G2V> just making a point.  It's a change in the role of, like, source code in our practice.

For example, Haskell is a programming language that was invented for use in papers, like PDFs, and only tangentially executable.

It's a matter of focus.  I'll be talking to theater people about AI soon and so I'm working a metaphor for them explaining what's happening to software development now.  And I think I've settled on a good one.

Software development can be like staging a play including the building of sets, the programming of lights, the buying of lights, building them from scratch, and building the stage, and the HVAC system for cooling the theater, and the whole building itself.

When it comes to the staging of a play, usually the HVAC is important but not creatively so.  (You could change the temperature to freak people out, but that's usually outside the magic circle of what a play does.)  So there's an excitement among some of us, different from the dread so present among other creatives, because a big chunk of what we do is dumb stuff we wish we weren't.

Starting with that there's a lot to elaborate on.
